hints: Apply LOTUS: p(z)=E[z^X]=Σ_k z^k P(X=k) with P(X=k)=(1−p)^{k−1}p.
It's a geometric series: p(z)=pz/(1−(1−p)z).
solution: By LOTUS, $p(z)=\sum_{k\ge1}z^k(1-p)^{k-1}p=\dfrac{pz}{1-(1-p)z}$. At $p=\tfrac23,\,z=\tfrac13$: numerator $\tfrac23\cdot\tfrac13=\tfrac29$, denominator $1-\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac13=\tfrac89$, so $p(\tfrac13)=\dfrac{2/9}{8/9}=\dfrac14.$

hints: Y−X is normal; find its variance via independence.
W=Y−X~N(0,5)=√5·Z, and E|Z|=√(2/π) for standard normal Z.
solution:
By independence $W=Y-X\sim N(0,1+4)=N(0,5)$, so $W=\sqrt5\,Z$ with $Z\sim N(0,1)$. Since $\mathbb{E}|Z|=\sqrt{2/\pi}$,
$$\mathbb{E}|Y-X|=\sqrt5\,\mathbb{E}|Z|=\sqrt5\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{\pi}}=\sqrt{\tfrac{10}{\pi}}=\Big(\tfrac{10}{\pi}\Big)^{1/2}.$$
So $K=10,\,b=\tfrac12$ and $bK=5.$

hints: Write A = J + 29I where J is the 30×30 all-ones matrix.
J has eigenvalues 30 (mult 1) and 0 (mult 29); add 29 to each ⇒ A has 59 (mult 1), 29 (mult 29).
A is symmetric so geometric=algebraic mult. λ+g=[59+1,29+29]=[60,58]; norm²=60²+58²=6964.
solution: Write $A=J+29I$ with $J$ the all-ones $30\times30$ matrix. $J$ has eigenvalue $30$ (multiplicity $1$, eigenvector $\mathbf 1$) and $0$ (multiplicity $29$). Adding $29I$ shifts these to $59$ (mult $1$) and $29$ (mult $29$). Being symmetric, geometric multiplicities equal algebraic ones, so $g=(1,29)$. Then $\lambda+g=(60,58)$ and $\|\lambda+g\|^2=60^2+58^2=3600+3364=6964.$

hints: Var(X+Y)=Var X+Var Y+2Cov, with Cov=ρσ_Xσ_Y.
solution: $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)=\rho\sigma_X\sigma_Y=-\tfrac38\cdot3\cdot4=-4.5$. Then $\mathrm{Var}(X+Y)=9+16+2(-4.5)=16$, so the standard deviation is $\sqrt{16}=4.$

hints: Send the two fastest to ferry the lantern; don't let the two slowest cross separately.
Cross the two slowest (10,5) together to avoid paying both.
solution: Label times Grace $1$, Frank $2$, Emma $5$, David $10$. Optimal plan: Grace+Frank cross ($2$), Grace returns ($1$), David+Emma cross ($10$), Frank returns ($2$), Grace+Frank cross ($2$). Total $=2+1+10+2+2=17$ minutes. The trick is to send the two slowest together so you pay $10$ only once.

hints: With n draws, payout = E[max] − cost = (1−1/(n+1)) − 0.05(n−1).
Maximize over n: derivative gives (n+1)²=20, so n≈3.47.
Check n=3 and n=4: both give 13/20.
Think about the repeating decimal of 1/7.
142857 is the repetend of 1/7; multiplying by 1–6 cyclically permutes its digits.
So 142857 — each product uses only {1,4,2,8,5,7}.
solution: The number $142857$ is the repeating block of $1/7$. Multiplying it by $1,2,3,4,5,6$ gives cyclic permutations of the same six digits ($142857,285714,428571,571428,714285,857142$), so no new digit ever appears. The answer is $142857.$

hints: Rerolling yields net E = 3.5 − 1 = 2.5, so keep any value above 2.5.
Reroll only on a 1 or 2; otherwise keep.
solution:
A reroll has expected net payout $3.5-1=2.5$, so Alice keeps her roll iff it exceeds $2.5$, i.e. she rerolls only on a $1$ or $2$. Then
$$\mathbb{E}=\tfrac16\big(\underbrace{2.5+2.5}_{\text{reroll on }1,2}+3+4+5+6\big)=\tfrac16(23)=\tfrac{23}{6}.$$

hints: You want pad 50 to be the LAST distinct pad visited.
On a cycle, by a gambler's-ruin/symmetry argument every non-start pad is equally likely to be the last visited.
There are 99 such pads, so the probability is 1/99.
solution: The event is that pad $50$ is the last of all pads to be visited. For a symmetric random walk on a cycle of $100$ pads started at $0$, a standard gambler's-ruin argument shows each of the $99$ non-start pads is equally likely to be the last one visited. Hence the probability is $\dfrac{1}{99}.$

hints: The minute hand gains on the hour hand at a constant relative rate.
Minute hand 360°/hr, hour hand 30°/hr ⇒ relative 330°/hr.
Meet again after 360/330 = 12/11 hours.
solution: The minute hand moves $360^\circ$/hr and the hour hand $30^\circ$/hr, so the minute hand gains $330^\circ$/hr. They meet again when it has gained a full $360^\circ$: $\dfrac{360}{330}=\dfrac{12}{11}$ hours.

hints: Condition on the first 2 flips: decided w.p. 3/4, restart (TT) w.p. 1/4.
Solve: E[N] = 8/3.
Track states by current progress toward HH; condition on the next flip(s).
Let E be from scratch: a T resets, H then T resets with one more, H then H ends.
solution:
Let $E$ be the expected flips from scratch. On the first flip: tails (½) wastes one flip and resets; heads (½) then either heads (¼ overall) ends after $2$ flips, or tails (¼ overall) wastes $2$ and resets. So
$$E=\tfrac12(E+1)+\tfrac14(E+2)+\tfrac14(2)\;\Rightarrow\;E=6.$$
